abstract:
Computer use agents automate digital tasks by directly interacting with
graphical user interfaces (GUIs) on computers and mobile devices, offering
significant potential to enhance human productivity by completing an open-ended
space of user queries. However, current agents face significant challenges:
imprecise grounding of GUI elements, difficulties with long-horizon task
planning, and performance bottlenecks from relying on single generalist models
for diverse cognitive tasks. To this end, we introduce Agent S2, a novel
compositional framework that delegates cognitive responsibilities across
various generalist and specialist models. We propose a novel
Mixture-of-Grounding technique to achieve precise GUI localization and
introduce Proactive Hierarchical Planning, dynamically refining action plans at
multiple temporal scales in response to evolving observations. Evaluations
demonstrate that Agent S2 establishes new state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance
on three prominent computer use benchmarks. Specifically, Agent S2 achieves
18.9% and 32.7% relative improvements over leading baseline agents such as
Claude Computer Use and UI-TARS on the OSWorld 15-step and 50-step evaluation.
Moreover, Agent S2 generalizes effectively to other operating systems and
applications, surpassing previous best methods by 52.8% on WindowsAgentArena
and by 16.52% on AndroidWorld relatively. Code available at
this https URL

keyInsights: A compositional framework integrating generalist and specialist models can outperform monolithic approaches for complex computer use tasks.
Dedicated specialist modules for GUI grounding (Mixture of Grounding) can significantly enhance precision and robustness, reducing common interaction errors.
Proactive hierarchical planning, which continuously refines subgoals, improves adaptability and robustness in dynamic, long-horizon task environments.
originalProblem: Imprecise GUI element grounding limits the reliability and accuracy of computer use agents, leading to frequent task failures.
Existing agents struggle with robust and adaptive long-horizon planning, particularly in dynamic environments with unexpected changes.
Monolithic generalist models face performance bottlenecks and lack efficiency when handling diverse cognitive tasks, such as both high-level reasoning and fine-grained GUI interaction.
results: Achieves new state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks, including OSWorld (e.g., 34.5% success on 50-step tasks), WindowsAgentArena (29.8% accuracy), and AndroidWorld (54.3% success).

Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of the Mixture of Grounding (MoG) and Proactive Hierarchical Planning (PHP), with MoG improving success rates by 3-4% and PHP by 4.6-6.15% on OSWorld subsets.

Error analysis indicates a shift in common failure modes from grounding to planning failures (41% of errors), demonstrating improved grounding capabilities and highlighting a new primary area for future work.
solution: Employs a compositional generalist-specialist framework, delegating high-level planning to a generalist Manager and tactical execution to a generalist Worker.
Introduces a "Mixture of Grounding" (MoG) with specialized experts (visual, textual, structural) for precise GUI element localization across different interaction types.
Implements "Proactive Hierarchical Planning" where the Manager continuously refines subgoals based on evolving observations, enabling adaptive long-horizon task completion.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Simular Research developed Agent S2, a compositional generalist-specialist framework for computer use agents that achieves new state-of-the-art performance on OSWorld, WindowsAgentArena, and AndroidWorld benchmarks. This framework utilizes a Mixture of Grounding experts for precise GUI interaction and Proactive Hierarchical Planning for robust, adaptive long-horizon task completion.

title: Agent S2: A Compositional Generalist-Specialist Framework for Computer  Use Agents

abstract: Recent advances in large language model (LLM) have empowered autonomous agents to perform complex tasks that require multi-turn interactions with tools and environments. However, scaling such agent training is limited by the lack of diverse and reliable environments. In this paper, we propose Agent World Model (AWM), a fully synthetic environment generation pipeline. Using this pipeline, we scale to 1,000 environments covering everyday scenarios, in which agents can interact with rich toolsets (35 tools per environment on average) and obtain high-quality observations. Notably, these environments are code-driven and backed by databases, providing more reliable and consistent state transitions than environments simulated by LLMs. Moreover, they enable more efficient agent interaction compared with collecting trajectories from realistic environments. To demonstrate the effectiveness of this resource, we perform large-scale reinforcement learning for multi-turn tool-use agents. Thanks to the fully executable environments and accessible database states, we can also design reliable reward functions. Experiments on three benchmarks show that training exclusively in synthetic environments, rather than benchmark-specific ones, yields strong out-of-distribution generalization. The code is available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

keyInsights: Code-driven and database-backed synthetic environments provide superior reliability and state consistency compared to purely LLM-simulated ones, which is crucial for effective and scalable reinforcement learning.

An iterative, execution-based self-correction loop is highly effective in enhancing the quality and robustness of LLM-generated code and environment components, achieving over 85% first-attempt success rates.

The 'code-augmented LLM-as-a-Judge' offers a more robust reward signal by effectively balancing the precision of code-based verification with the contextual understanding of LLMs, outperforming singular approaches.
originalProblem: A severe scarcity of diverse, reliable, and efficient training environments hinders the scalable development of LLM-powered autonomous agents via reinforcement learning.

Existing real-world environments are expensive and lack stability, while human-crafted environments are limited in scale and diversity; LLM-simulated environments suffer from unreliability due to hallucinations and high inference costs.
A need for consistent state transitions and efficient interaction capabilities that can support thousands of reinforcement learning training steps.
results: AWM successfully synthesized 1,000 unique environments with an average of 35 tools each (totaling 35,062 tools) and 10,000 diverse tasks, establishing the largest open-source tool-use environment set to date.

Agents trained exclusively on AWM's synthetic environments consistently outperformed baseline LLMs and agents trained in LLM-simulated environments across three distinct, out-of-distribution real-world benchmarks (BFCLv3, τ2-bench, MCP-Universe).

The study demonstrated that increasing the scale and diversity of training environments leads to a monotonic improvement in agent performance and generalization capabilities, validating the importance of large-scale synthetic data.
solution: Developed the Agent World Model (AWM) pipeline, which uses LLMs to synthesize 1,000 executable, code-driven tool-use environments with database-backed (SQLite) state, complete with diverse scenarios, tasks, and Python backend code.

Integrated an execution-based self-correction mechanism that iteratively feeds back compilation and runtime errors to the LLM for refinement of synthesized database schemas, interface code, and verification functions.

Implemented a 'code-augmented LLM-as-a-Judge' verification module that combines structured, code-based state checks with LLM reasoning to provide robust and reliable reward signals for reinforcement learning.
paper_summary.summary: The Agent World Model (AWM) introduces an open-source, code-driven pipeline that synthesizes 1,000 diverse, executable tool-use environments with database-backed state for large-scale reinforcement learning of autonomous agents. Agents trained on these synthetic environments, developed collaboratively by UNC Chapel Hill and Snowflake, demonstrate strong out-of-distribution generalization across various real-world benchmarks, validating the approach for developing robust LLM agents.

title: Agent World Model: Infinity Synthetic Environments for Agentic Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Vision-language models have demonstrated impressive capabilities as computer-use agents (CUAs) capable of automating diverse computer tasks. As their commercial potential grows, critical details of the most capable CUA systems remain closed. As these agents will increasingly mediate digital interactions and execute consequential decisions on our behalf, the research community needs access to open CUA frameworks to study their capabilities, limitations, and risks. To bridge this gap, we propose OpenCUA, a comprehensive open-source framework for scaling CUA data and foundation models. Our framework consists of: (1) an annotation infrastructure that seamlessly captures human computer-use demonstrations; (2) AgentNet, the first large-scale computer-use task dataset spanning 3 operating systems and 200+ applications and websites; (3) a scalable pipeline that transforms demonstrations into state-action pairs with reflective long Chain-of-Thought reasoning that sustain robust performance gains as data scales. Our end-to-end agent models demonstrate strong performance across CUA benchmarks. In particular, OpenCUA-72B achieves an average success rate of 45.0% on OSWorld-Verified, establishing a new state-of-the-art (SOTA) among open-source models. Further analysis confirms that our approach generalizes well across domains and benefits significantly from increased test-time computation. We release our annotation tool, datasets, code, and models to build open foundations for further CUA research.

organization_info.name: XLANG Lab, University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Reflective L2 Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning, with planning and error recovery, significantly boosts agent performance at inference time compared to simpler CoT formats.
Performance of computer-use agents scales positively with both the volume and diversity of training data, and cross-platform training improves generalization across different operating systems.
Strong GUI grounding is necessary but not sufficient for complex agentic tasks; high-level planning and reflective reasoning are crucial for reliable task completion and error detection.
originalProblem: Most capable computer-use agent (CUA) systems are proprietary and closed-source, hindering research into their capabilities, limitations, and risks.
There is a lack of scalable, open-source infrastructure for collecting diverse, large-scale computer-use data, including real-time user interactions and accessibility trees.
Existing open-source CUA datasets are limited in scope, scale, and diversity, focusing on specific domains rather than general computer-use applications.
results: OPENCUA-72B achieved an average success rate of 45.0% on OSWorld-Verified (100 steps), establishing a new state-of-the-art among open-source CUA models.
OPENCUA models demonstrated state-of-the-art performance on mainstream GUI-grounding benchmarks, achieving 37.3% on UI-Vision and 60.8% on ScreenSpot-Pro.
The AGENTNETBENCH offline benchmark shows strong correlation with online OSWorld leaderboard rankings, validating its utility for efficient CUA development and evaluation.
solution: Developed AGENTNET TOOL, a cross-platform application for seamlessly capturing human computer-use demonstrations (screen videos, mouse/keyboard signals, accessibility trees) across Windows, macOS, and Ubuntu.
Constructed AGENTNET, the first large-scale (22,625 trajectories) and diverse computer-use task dataset spanning multiple operating systems and over 200 applications/websites.

Proposed a scalable training pipeline incorporating novel reflective long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning synthesis and a multi-image history context encoding for improved agent planning and error recovery.
paper_summary.summary: The OPENCUA framework provides open foundations for computer-use agents, releasing tools for data collection, a large-scale dataset of 22,625 trajectories across multiple operating systems, and models. Its OPENCUA-72B model achieved 45.0% success on the OSWorld-Verified benchmark, representing a new state-of-the-art for open-source systems and reducing the performance gap with proprietary agents.

title: OpenCUA: Open Foundations for Computer-Use Agents

abstract: Computer-use agents (CUAs) hold promise for automating everyday digital tasks, but their performance on long-horizon, complex problems remains unreliable. Single-rollout execution is brittle, with small errors compounding over time and leading to high variance in outcomes. While prior work has attempted to scale within a single rollout, such approaches have yielded limited gains. Scaling over multiple rollouts offers a more promising alternative but doing so effectively is challenging due to the difficulty of evaluating and selecting among long-horizon agent behaviors. We introduce Behavior Judge (BJudge), which addresses this challenge by representing agent executions as behavior narratives and comparing candidate behaviors at this level, substantially improving robustness and success rates. Using multiple rollouts, BJudge establishes a new state of the art (SoTA) in OSWorld at 72.6%, significantly outperforming prior methods and surpassing human-level performance at 72.36%, with comprehensive ablations validating key design choices. We further demonstrate strong generalization results to different operating systems on WindowsAgentArena and AndroidWorld. Crucially, our results highlight the strong effectiveness of scaling CUAs, when you do it right: effective scaling requires structured trajectory understanding and selection, and BJudge provides a practical framework to achieve this.

keyInsights: Wide scaling, enabled by a principled trajectory evaluation framework, is highly effective in improving the reliability and success rates of CUAs on long-horizon tasks.
Abstracting dense raw trajectories into concise 'behavior narratives' effectively filters irrelevant details, making VLM-based comparative evaluation tractable and robust.
A strong base agent (Agent S3) that integrates coding capabilities and employs a flat planning policy significantly improves initial rollout quality, complementing the wide scaling approach.
originalProblem: Computer-Use Agents (CUAs) exhibit unreliability and high variance on complex, long-horizon tasks due to compounding errors in single execution trajectories.
Traditional 'deep scaling' methods (improving single-trajectory planning) yield limited gains for CUAs.
The 'wide scaling' approach (generating multiple rollouts) for CUAs is bottlenecked by the difficulty of reliably evaluating and selecting the best trajectory among many candidates.
results: BJudge achieved a new state-of-the-art 72.6% success rate on the OSWorld benchmark (100-step tasks), surpassing human performance of 72.36% and improving 9.2% over previous best methods.
Behavior Narratives proved effective, improving the success rate by 3.4% over naive captioning in ablation studies, validating their role in trajectory representation.

The framework demonstrated strong zero-shot generalization, yielding 6.4% and 3.5% improvements in success rates on WindowsAgentArena and AndroidWorld, respectively, compared to the Agent S3 baseline.
solution: The Behavior Judge (BJudge) framework enables wide scaling for CUAs, comprising an improved base agent (Agent S3), a Behavior Narrative Generator, and a Comparative Behavior Evaluator.
The Behavior Narrative Generator converts raw, dense agent trajectories into concise 'behavior narratives' by using a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to extract task-relevant action-effect facts.

The Comparative Behavior Evaluator uses a VLM in a single-round multiple-choice question format to simultaneously compare and select the best trajectory from multiple candidates based on their behavior narratives.
paper_summary.summary: Simular Research introduces Behavior Judge (BJudge), a framework enabling effective wide scaling for Computer-Use Agents (CUAs) by generating and evaluating multiple execution trajectories. BJudge achieved a 72.6% success rate on the OSWorld benchmark, establishing a new state-of-the-art and surpassing human-level performance of 72.36%.

title: Scaling Agents for Computer Use

abstract: AI agents may soon become capable of autonomously completing valuable, long-horizon tasks in diverse domains. Current benchmarks either do not measure real-world tasks, or are not sufficiently difficult to meaningfully measure frontier models. To this end, we present Terminal-Bench 2.0: a carefully curated hard benchmark composed of 89 tasks in computer terminal environments inspired by problems from real workflows. Each task features a unique environment, human-written solution, and comprehensive tests for verification. We show that frontier models and agents score less than 65\% on the benchmark and conduct an error analysis to identify areas for model and agent improvement. We publish the dataset and evaluation harness to assist developers and researchers in future work at this https URL .

keyInsights: Terminal-Bench 2.0 proves to be a highly challenging benchmark, with frontier models achieving resolution rates below 65%, highlighting significant room for improvement in current AI agent capabilities.
Model selection is more critical than the specific agent scaffold, with proprietary models consistently outperforming open-weight models and showing rapid performance improvement over time.

Error analysis reveals that 'Execution errors' (e.g., disobeying instructions, step repetition) are the dominant failure mode for advanced closed-source models, while 'Command not found' is a frequent command-level issue.
originalProblem: Existing AI agent benchmarks often lack the realism and sufficient difficulty needed to accurately evaluate frontier models on complex, real-world tasks.

There is a need for a standardized and robust evaluation framework specifically for command-line interfaces, a ubiquitous environment for highly skilled technical work, that goes beyond simple coding or web interaction tasks.
Prior benchmarks frequently focus on isolated capabilities, failing to assess an agent's ability to perform long-horizon, interactive problem-solving requiring extensive domain knowledge.
results: GPT-5.2 with Codex CLI achieved the highest average resolution rate at 62.9%, followed by Claude Opus 4.5 with Terminus 2 (57.8%), demonstrating the benchmark's difficulty for state-of-the-art systems.
The performance of state-of-the-art models nearly doubled within an eight-month period, indicating a rapid advancement in AI agent capabilities.

A weak correlation was observed between efficiency metrics (e.g., number of turns or output tokens) and success rate, suggesting that quality of reasoning per interaction is more important than verbosity or numerous attempts.
solution: Researchers developed Terminal-Bench 2.0, a benchmark comprising 89 challenging tasks across diverse domains, conducted in isolated, containerized command-line environments.
The evaluation framework is outcome-driven, using programmatic tests to verify task completion by checking the final state of the container, rather than prescribing specific agent actions.
A rigorous, multi-stage verification process, involving human experts, automated checks, LLM-backed tools, and adversarial audits, ensures the quality, solvability, and integrity of each task.
paper_summary.summary: Terminal-Bench 2.0 introduces a benchmark to evaluate AI agents on 89 hard, realistic tasks within command-line environments, revealing that even frontier models achieve a resolution rate of less than 65%. This work establishes a challenging standard for assessing agent capabilities in complex technical workflows.

title: Terminal-Bench: Benchmarking Agents on Hard, Realistic Tasks in Command Line Interfaces

abstract:
Autonomous agents that accomplish complex computer tasks with minimal human
interventions have the potential to transform human-computer interaction,
significantly enhancing accessibility and productivity. However, existing
benchmarks either lack an interactive environment or are limited to
environments specific to certain applications or domains, failing to reflect
the diverse and complex nature of real-world computer use, thereby limiting the
scope of tasks and agent scalability. To address this issue, we introduce
OSWorld, the first-of-its-kind scalable, real computer environment for
multimodal agents, supporting task setup, execution-based evaluation, and
interactive learning across various operating systems such as Ubuntu, Windows,
and macOS. OSWorld can serve as a unified, integrated computer environment for
assessing open-ended computer tasks that involve arbitrary applications.
Building upon OSWorld, we create a benchmark of 369 computer tasks involving
real web and desktop apps in open domains, OS file I/O, and workflows spanning
multiple applications. Each task example is derived from real-world computer
use cases and includes a detailed initial state setup configuration and a
custom execution-based evaluation script for reliable, reproducible evaluation.
Extensive evaluation of state-of-the-art LLM/VLM-based agents on OSWorld
reveals significant deficiencies in their ability to serve as computer
assistants. While humans can accomplish over 72.36% of the tasks, the best
model achieves only 12.24% success, primarily struggling with GUI grounding and
operational knowledge. Comprehensive analysis using OSWorld provides valuable
insights for developing multimodal generalist agents that were not possible
with previous benchmarks. Our code, environment, baseline models, and data are
publicly available at https://os-world.github.io.

keyInsights: The accessibility tree provides crucial structured information that aids vision-language models (VLMs) in spatial grounding, leading to improved performance compared to raw screenshots alone.
Current VLMs struggle to effectively leverage visual history for contextual understanding in long-horizon tasks, as additional screenshot history did not enhance performance.
Agent performance is highly sensitive to UI layout perturbations (e.g., window position, size, clutter), revealing a lack of robustness and strategic window management.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for autonomous agents lack interactive environments, rely on static datasets, or are confined to specific domains (e.g., web, coding).
Current evaluation methods are often not execution-based, leading to incomplete or inaccurate assessments that penalize valid alternative solutions.
Prior benchmarks fail to capture the open-ended, multi-application, and multi-interface nature of real-world computer usage across diverse operating systems.
results: State-of-the-art agents achieve a maximum success rate of 12.24% on OSWORLD tasks, significantly trailing human performance of 72.36%.
Multi-application workflow tasks pose a major challenge for agents, with success rates of only 6.57% compared to 13.74% for single-app tasks.
Common failure modes include mouse click inaccuracies (over 75% of failures for GPT-4V), struggles with environmental noise (pop-ups), and limited domain knowledge for specialized software.
solution: OSWORLD leverages virtual machine techniques to create a scalable real computer environment supporting Ubuntu, Windows, and macOS, with a universal PyAutoGUI-based action space for full human-computer interaction.
It provides rich multimodal observations to agents, including complete screenshots and XML-format accessibility trees, along with terminal output.
A benchmark of 369 real-world tasks on Ubuntu (and 43 on Windows) was constructed with detailed initial state setups and custom execution-based evaluation scripts for objective verification.
paper_summary.summary: OSWORLD, developed by The University of Hong Kong and collaborators including Salesforce Research, introduces the first scalable benchmark for evaluating multimodal AI agents in real computer environments. It reveals a substantial performance gap between state-of-the-art models and humans on open-ended tasks, with agents achieving only 12.24% success compared to humans' 72.36%.

title:
OSWorld: Benchmarking Multimodal Agents for Open-Ended Tasks in Real
  Computer Environments

abstract: Real-world language agents must handle complex, multi-step workflows across diverse Apps. For instance, an agent may manage emails by coordinating with calendars and file systems, or monitor a production database to detect anomalies and generate reports following an operating manual. However, existing language agent benchmarks often focus on narrow domains or simplified tasks that lack the diversity, realism, and long-horizon complexity required to evaluate agents' real-world performance. To address this gap, we introduce the Tool Decathlon (dubbed as Toolathlon), a benchmark for language agents offering diverse Apps and tools, realistic environment setup, and reliable execution-based evaluation. Toolathlon spans 32 software applications and 604 tools, ranging from everyday platforms such as Google Calendar and Notion to professional ones like WooCommerce, Kubernetes, and BigQuery. Most of the tools are based on a high-quality set of Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers that we may have revised or implemented ourselves. Unlike prior works, which primarily ensure functional realism but offer limited environment state diversity, we provide realistic initial environment states from real software, such as Canvas courses with dozens of students or real financial spreadsheets. This benchmark includes 108 manually sourced or crafted tasks in total, requiring interacting with multiple Apps over around 20 turns on average to complete. Each task is strictly verifiable through dedicated evaluation scripts. Comprehensive evaluation of SOTA models highlights their significant shortcomings: the best-performing model, Claude-4.5-Sonnet, achieves only a 38.6% success rate with 20.2 tool calling turns on average, while the top open-weights model DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp reaches 20.1%. We expect Toolathlon to drive the development of more capable language agents for real-world, long-horizon task execution.

keyInsights: Effective exploration of observations and environment states appears more critical for agentic tasks than increased internal reasoning, as models often perform better by leveraging environmental feedback.
Handling overlong tool outputs (e.g., extensive HTML) and managing long-context information remains a significant challenge for most models, contributing to reduced success rates.

The discrepancy between potential capability (Pass@3) and consistent reliability (Pass^3) highlights that while models may sometimes solve tasks, they often lack the dependability required for real-world application.
originalProblem: Existing language agent benchmarks often exhibit a restricted focus, covering narrow domains or simplified tasks, and lack the diversity, realism, and long-horizon complexity required for practical evaluation.
Prior evaluation methods frequently rely on synthetic environments, simplified initial states, or subjective Language Model (LLM) judges, which can compromise reliability and reproducibility.
Current benchmarks typically do not require agents to orchestrate operations across multiple disparate real-world applications or interpret ambiguous, fuzzy user instructions.
results: State-of-the-art language models show substantial limitations, with the best-performing model, Claude-4.5-Sonnet, achieving only a 38.6% success rate, and the top open-weight model, DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp, reaching 20.1% on TOOLATHLON's tasks.

Proprietary models generally outperformed open-source models, with Claude-4.5-Sonnet demonstrating strong general capabilities and other models showing specialized strengths across different task categories.

Analysis revealed common failure modes including tool execution errors (e.g., incorrect parameter passing), challenges with long-context processing, and premature task termination, indicating key areas for future agent robustness and reliability improvements.
solution: The Tool Decathlon (TOOLATHLON) benchmark was developed with 108 manually sourced or crafted tasks, encompassing 32 real-world software applications and 604 tools, designed for diversity, realism, and long-horizon complexity.
A hybrid environment setup utilizes remote applications and locally containerized open-source software to provide realistic initial states and enable scalable, complex state simulation.

A strictly verifiable, execution-based evaluation framework with unique, manually crafted scripts for each task ensures precise and consistent measurement of success against static or dynamically generated ground-truth states.
paper_summary.summary: The Tool Decathlon (TOOLATHLON) is introduced as a new benchmark for evaluating language agents on diverse, realistic, and long-horizon tasks spanning 32 real-world applications. Evaluations reveal that state-of-the-art models, including Claude-4.5-Sonnet, achieve only up to a 38.6% success rate, underscoring significant limitations in current agent capabilities for complex, multi-step operations.

title: The Tool Decathlon: Benchmarking Language Agents for Diverse, Realistic, and Long-Horizon Task Execution

abstract:
Autonomous agents that navigate Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs) to automate
tasks like document editing and file management can greatly enhance computer
workflows. While existing research focuses on online settings, desktop
environments, critical for many professional and everyday tasks, remain
underexplored due to data collection challenges and licensing issues. We
introduce UI-Vision, the first comprehensive, license-permissive benchmark for
offline, fine-grained evaluation of computer use agents in real-world desktop
environments. Unlike online benchmarks, UI-Vision provides: (i) dense,
high-quality annotations of human demonstrations, including bounding boxes, UI
labels, and action trajectories (clicks, drags, and keyboard inputs) across 83
software applications, and (ii) three fine-to-coarse grained tasks-Element
Grounding, Layout Grounding, and Action Prediction-with well-defined metrics to
rigorously evaluate agents' performance in desktop environments. Our evaluation
reveals critical limitations in state-of-the-art models like UI-TARS-72B,
including issues with understanding professional software, spatial reasoning,
and complex actions like drag-and-drop. These findings highlight the challenges
in developing fully autonomous computer use agents. By releasing UI-Vision as
open-source, we aim to advance the development of more capable agents for
real-world desktop tasks.

keyInsights: Current state-of-the-art models, including specialized GUI agents and general VLMs, demonstrate limited accuracy in fine-grained element grounding, especially on small elements, dense UIs, and spatial reasoning tasks.

Closed-source Vision-Language Models (VLMs) show stronger performance in the novel Layout Grounding task compared to many open-source GUI agents, indicating a general visual understanding is beneficial for semantic UI layout comprehension.
Evaluation reveals that accurate action prediction is primarily bottlenecked by precise grounding of actions within the UI rather than the planning of the actions themselves.
originalProblem: Desktop Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs) are underexplored for autonomous agents, lacking structured data like web DOMs and facing data collection/licensing challenges.
Existing GUI benchmarks are predominantly designed for web or mobile environments, failing to capture the unique visual and interaction complexities of desktop applications.
Current models struggle with fine-grained visual parsing, spatial reasoning, and complex interaction patterns (e.g., drag-and-drop) required for effective desktop automation.
results: On Element Grounding, the best closed-source VLM, Claude-3.7-Sonnet, achieved 8.27% accuracy, while the top specialized GUI agent, UI-TARS-72B, reached 25.5%, indicating significant room for improvement.
Gemini-1.5-Pro achieved the highest IoU of 30.8% on Layout Grounding, notably outperforming several dedicated GUI agents and highlighting VLMs' strength in semantic layout comprehension.

In Action Prediction, UI-TARS-7B recorded the highest Step Success Rate (SSR) at 21.4%, but all evaluated models showed persistent struggles with precise click and drag actions, particularly in visually dense or functionally rich applications.
solution: Introduced UI-Vision, a comprehensive, license-permissive benchmark built from 83 real-world open-source desktop applications across six domains.
Curated a dataset of 450 high-quality human demonstrations with dense annotations, including bounding boxes for all UI elements, descriptive labels, and precise action trajectories.

Established three fine-to-coarse grained evaluation tasks: Element Grounding, Layout Grounding (novel), and Action Prediction, to assess visual perception, spatial reasoning, and complex interaction skills.
paper_summary.summary: UI-Vision is a comprehensive, license-permissive benchmark for evaluating autonomous agents in real-world desktop Graphical User Interfaces, featuring dense annotations across 83 open-source applications. It provides tasks for element grounding, layout grounding, and action prediction, demonstrating that current state-of-the-art models face significant challenges in fine-grained visual perception and complex interactions on desktop environments.

title: UI-Vision: A Desktop-centric GUI Benchmark for Visual Perception and  Interaction

abstract: Among existing online mobile-use benchmarks, AndroidWorld has emerged as the dominant benchmark due to its reproducible environment and deterministic evaluation; however, recent agents achieving over 90% success rates indicate its saturation and motivate the need for a more challenging benchmark. In addition, its environment lacks key application categories, such as e-commerce and enterprise communication, and does not reflect realistic mobile-use scenarios characterized by vague user instructions and hybrid tool usage. We introduce MobileWorld, a substantially more challenging benchmark designed to reflect real-world usage through 201 tasks across 20 applications. MobileWorld derives its difficulty from an emphasis on long-horizon, cross-application workflows, requiring nearly twice as many completion steps on average (27.8 vs. 14.3) and featuring a significantly higher proportion of multi-app tasks (62.2% vs. 9.5%) than AndroidWorld. To overcome the limitations of existing environments, MobileWorld achieves a balance between production-grade utility and reproducible evaluation by utilizing open-source alternatives to industry standards (e.g., Mattermost for Slack). This approach enables a fully observable and controlled environment through source code modification and direct backend database access for precise verification. MobileWorld also introduces novel task categories, including agent-user interaction and Model Context Protocol (MCP)-augmented tasks, for evaluating agents in user-aware, hybrid-tool scenarios. To facilitate evaluation, we develop a planner-executor agentic framework with extended action spaces to support user interactions and MCP calls. Our results reveal a sharp performance drop compared to AndroidWorld, with the best agentic framework and end-to-end model achieving 51.7% and 20.9% success rates, respectively, highlighting ample headroom for future research.

keyInsights: MobileWorld tasks are significantly more complex than previous benchmarks, requiring nearly twice as many completion steps (27.8 vs. 14.3) and featuring a substantially higher proportion of multi-application tasks (62.2% vs. 9.5%).

Agentic frameworks, which combine large multimodal models for planning with specialized grounding executors for GUI interaction, consistently and significantly outperform end-to-end GUI-specialized models.

Current state-of-the-art mobile agents demonstrate critical deficiencies in detecting ambiguity to initiate user interaction, effectively managing external tool outputs, long-term memory, and executing complex logical reasoning over multiple steps.
originalProblem: Existing mobile GUI benchmarks, like AndroidWorld, are saturated, with state-of-the-art agents achieving over 90% success, limiting their ability to differentiate agent capabilities.
Previous benchmarks lack real-world complexity, omitting crucial application categories, focusing on short-horizon single-application tasks, and assuming fully-specified user instructions.
Many benchmarks using commercial applications suffer from non-deterministic evaluation due to opaque internal states and authentication issues, often relying on stochastic "MLLM-as-a-judge" methods.
results: The best-performing agent, based on the GPT-5 model within an agentic framework, achieved only a 51.7% overall success rate on MobileWorld, indicating substantial headroom for future research.

End-to-end models showed severe limitations in agent-user interaction tasks (scoring below 10%) and MCP-augmented tasks (scoring near 0%), primarily due to architectural and reasoning gaps in handling clarification and tool invocation.

Manual error analysis identified core challenges, including agents hallucinating missing information instead of querying the user, context window overflow from lengthy MCP tool outputs, and poor long-term state tracking leading to repetitive or destructive actions.
solution: MobileWorld introduces a new benchmark comprising 201 long-horizon, cross-application tasks across 20 production-grade, self-hosted open-source applications (e.g., Mattermost, Mastodon, Mall4Uni).

The benchmark integrates two novel task paradigms: agent-user interaction, where agents proactively clarify ambiguous instructions, and Model Context Protocol (MCP)-augmented tasks, requiring agents to invoke external tools in hybrid GUI/API workflows.

A robust, containerized Docker-in-Docker environment with Android Virtual Device (AVD) snapshots and multi-faceted deterministic verification (backend DB, local storage, app callbacks) ensures reproducible and reliable evaluation.
paper_summary.summary: MobileWorld establishes a new benchmark for autonomous mobile agents, integrating agent-user interaction and Model Context Protocol (MCP)-augmented tasks within a containerized, deterministically evaluated environment. State-of-the-art models achieve an overall success rate of 51.7%, revealing current limitations in complex reasoning, multi-turn dialogue, and external tool utilization.

title: MobileWorld: Benchmarking Autonomous Mobile Agents in Agent-User Interactive and MCP-Augmented Environments

abstract:
Tool use has turned large language models (LLMs) into powerful agents that
can perform complex multi-step tasks by dynamically utilising external software
components. However, these tools must be implemented in advance by human
developers, hindering the applicability of LLM agents in domains demanding
large numbers of highly specialised tools, like in life sciences and medicine.
Motivated by the growing trend of scientific studies accompanied by public code
repositories, we propose ToolMaker, an agentic framework that autonomously
transforms papers with code into LLM-compatible tools. Given a GitHub URL and
short task description, ToolMaker autonomously installs dependencies and
generates code to perform the task, using a closed-loop self-correction
mechanism for debugging. To evaluate our approach, we introduce a benchmark
comprising 15 complex computational tasks spanning various domains with over
100 unit tests to assess correctness and robustness. Our method correctly
implements 80% of the tasks, substantially outperforming current
state-of-the-art software engineering agents. ToolMaker therefore is a step
towards fully autonomous agent-based scientific workflows. Our code and
benchmark are publicly available at this https URL

keyInsights: LLM agents can transition from mere tool users to autonomous tool creators, leveraging existing, complex scientific software rather than building simple functions from scratch.
Robust, containerized environment setup combined with iterative self-correction is critical for successfully integrating complex scientific code with diverse dependencies.
The vast repository of publicly available scientific code offers a scalable and pragmatic resource for dynamically expanding LLM agent capabilities in specialized domains.
originalProblem: Large Language Model (LLM) agents are fundamentally limited by their reliance on human-predefined tools, especially in specialized and data-intensive scientific domains.
Manual development and integration of the vast, specialized computational tools required in fields like life sciences and medicine is a prohibitive bottleneck.
Existing LLM-based tool creation methods typically build simple tools from scratch, often lacking essential operating system interaction and the ability to handle complex computational dependencies.
results: TOOLMAKER successfully implemented 80% (12 out of 15) of complex scientific tasks on the new TM-BENCH benchmark, compared to an adapted OpenHands baseline which achieved only 20% success (3 out of 15).
The framework demonstrated the ability to create sophisticated, multi-step tools, managing complex dependencies and performing an average of 21.8 actions per tool creation.

While TOOLMAKER incurred a higher average cost per tool ($0.94 vs. $0.15 for OpenHands), this was justified by its vastly superior success rate on complex scientific problems that leading software engineering agents failed to solve.
solution: Develop TOOLMAKER, an agentic framework that autonomously converts publicly available scientific code repositories (from "papers with code") into robust, LLM-compatible tools.
Implement a two-stage agentic workflow comprising autonomous environment setup (generating a reproducible Docker image) and tool implementation (generating a Python function to wrap existing code).
Integrate a closed-loop self-correction mechanism where the agent iteratively tests, diagnoses errors, and refines its generated tool within a sandboxed, reset environment.
paper_summary.summary: This research introduces TOOLMAKER, an agentic framework developed by a diverse team including KatherLab, that autonomously transforms existing scientific code repositories into LLM-compatible tools, enabling agents to dynamically expand their capabilities beyond human-predefined functions. It achieves an 80% success rate on complex scientific tasks on the new TM-BENCH benchmark, significantly outperforming other state-of-the-art software engineering agents.

title: LLM Agents Making Agent Tools

abstract: Autonomous agents powered by large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in real-world applications requiring complex, long-horizon workflows. However, existing benchmarks predominantly focus on atomic tasks that are self-contained and independent, failing to capture the long-term contextual dependencies and multi-interaction coordination required in realistic scenarios. To address this gap, we introduce OdysseyBench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on long-horizon workflows across diverse office applications including Word, Excel, PDF, Email, and Calendar. Our benchmark comprises two complementary splits: OdysseyBench+ with 300 tasks derived from real-world use cases, and OdysseyBench-Neo with 302 newly synthesized complex tasks. Each task requires agent to identify essential information from long-horizon interaction histories and perform multi-step reasoning across various applications. To enable scalable benchmark creation, we propose HomerAgents, a multi-agent framework that automates the generation of long-horizon workflow benchmarks through systematic environment exploration, task generation, and dialogue synthesis. Our extensive evaluation demonstrates that OdysseyBench effectively challenges state-of-the-art LLM agents, providing more accurate assessment of their capabilities in complex, real-world contexts compared to existing atomic task benchmarks. We believe that OdysseyBench will serve as a valuable resource for advancing the development and evaluation of LLM agents in real-world productivity scenarios. In addition, we release OdysseyBench and HomerAgents to foster research along this line.

keyInsights: LLM agent performance degrades substantially as the number of office applications involved in a task increases, highlighting challenges in multi-tool orchestration and state management.

Semantic summarization of dialogue history, particularly at the chunk level, improves agent performance on long-horizon tasks, often surpassing full context provision while using significantly fewer tokens.
The quality and relevance of retrieved context are more critical than the sheer volume, as increasing top-k retrieved documents beyond an optimal point can introduce noise and decrease performance.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent benchmarks predominantly focus on self-contained, atomic tasks, failing to capture real-world workflow complexity.
There is a lack of evaluation for LLM agents on long-horizon, multi-step tasks requiring sustained interaction, contextual understanding, and coordination across diverse office applications.
Creating such complex benchmarks through human annotation is costly and not scalable, limiting task diversity.
results: State-of-the-art LLM agents exhibited significant performance degradation, with average success rates dropping substantially (e.g., 72.83% to 30.36% for one model) when tasks involved more applications.

Semantic context summarization, especially chunk-level, consistently improved agent performance (e.g., GPT-4o achieved 56.29% on OdysseyBench-Neo) while requiring less than 20% of the tokens compared to long-context baselines.

Error analysis identified common failure patterns including inaccurate planning, incorrect tool calls, and difficulty with file creation or modification across various office applications, particularly `.docx` and `.xlsx`.
solution: Introduced OdysseyBench, a two-split benchmark (OdysseyBench+ and OdysseyBench-Neo) featuring long-horizon, multi-application office workflows spanning Word, Excel, PDF, Email, and Calendar.
Developed HOMERAGENTS, a multi-agent framework utilizing GPT-4.1 to automatically generate diverse and complex long-horizon tasks and their corresponding multi-day dialogue histories.

Implemented and evaluated state-of-the-art LLM agents on OdysseyBench with different context management strategies, including long-context and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) using raw versus semantically summarized context.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers introduced OdysseyBench, a benchmark designed to evaluate LLM agents on long-horizon, complex workflows across multiple office applications, revealing that current agents face challenges in multi-application coordination but perform better when provided with semantically summarized context. This approach improved agent performance on complex tasks while substantially reducing token requirements.

title: OdysseyBench: Evaluating LLM Agents on Long-Horizon Complex Office Application Workflows

abstract: Computer-using agents (CUAs) must plan task workflows across diverse and evolving applications, yet progress is limited by the lack of large-scale, high-quality training data. Existing datasets are narrow, static, and costly to annotate, while synthetic data often yields oversimplified or misaligned behaviors. We present Watch & Learn (W&L), a framework that converts readily available Internet videos of human computer use into executable UI trajectories at scale. Instead of directly generating actions or relying on handcrafted heuristics, we cast trajectory annotation as an inverse dynamics problem that predicts user actions from consecutive screen states, which simplifies learning and generalizes across domains. Through a task-aware retrieval and labeling pipeline, W&L yields over 53K high-quality trajectories that enhance CUAs both as in-context exemplars and as supervised training data. On OSWorld, it consistently improves general-purpose and specialized CUAs, while on WindowsAgentArena it achieves state-of-the-art performance among 7B-scale models under the 15-step limit. These results show that web-scale human demonstration videos can serve as a practical and scalable foundation for advancing real-world CUAs.

keyInsights: Formulating trajectory recovery as an inverse dynamics problem, directly predicting actions from screen state transitions, leads to significantly higher action labeling accuracy than prior heuristic-based or MLLM-dependent pipelines.

Web-scale human demonstration videos, when processed by W&L's IDM, provide a scalable and high-quality data source that effectively enhances both in-context learning and supervised fine-tuning for CUAs across different operating systems.

The benefits of supervised fine-tuning with W&L's trajectories show disproportionately larger gains with increased data scale, indicating that a critical mass of high-quality data is essential for robust CUA learning capabilities.
originalProblem: High cost and time constraints of manually collecting large-scale, high-quality human demonstration data for training robust Computer-Using Agents (CUAs).
Limitations of existing data synthesis methods, which often suffer from cascading errors in offline reconstruction or yield low task complexity and high acquisition costs in online exploration.
The untapped potential of vast, readily available online human demonstration videos (e.g., tutorials, screencasts) as a scalable source of structured, executable action trajectories.
results: W&L-labeled trajectories improved CUA success rates on OSWorld by +1.6 to +3.0 percentage points for in-context learning and up to +11.1 points for supervised fine-tuning (Qwen 2.5-VL 7B), and achieved a +5.9 point increase for UI-TARS-1.5-7B on WindowsAgentArena.

The Inverse Dynamics Model (IDM) achieved 95.8% ActionType Accuracy and 91.7% Action Accuracy on a held-out test set, significantly outperforming Gemini 2.5 Flash and TongUI's labeling pipeline, which directly correlated with improved downstream CUA performance.

Ablation studies confirmed that the cumulative addition of action labels and natural language reasoning derived from W&L trajectories enhanced in-context learning, with task-relevant video retrieval further boosting performance by +2.5 points on OSWorld.
solution: Developed the Watch & Learn (W&L) framework to automatically convert web-scale human demonstration videos into structured, executable UI trajectories without manual annotation.
Trained an Inverse Dynamics Model (IDM) to predict user actions directly from consecutive screen observations, framing trajectory annotation as an inverse dynamics problem.

Utilized the automatically generated trajectories in a dual role: as in-context exemplars for general-purpose agents and as supervised training data for fine-tuning multimodal Large Language Models (LLMs).
paper_summary.summary: The Watch & Learn (W&L) framework facilitates the conversion of web-scale human demonstration videos into executable UI trajectories for Computer-Using Agents (CUAs). By employing an Inverse Dynamics Model to predict user actions from consecutive screen states, the method improves CUA performance on interactive benchmarks, including an +11.1 percentage point increase for the Qwen 2.5-VL 7B model through supervised fine-tuning.

title: Watch and Learn: Learning to Use Computers from Online Videos

abstract: Progress in computer use agents (CUAs) has been constrained by the absence of large and high-quality datasets that capture how humans interact with a computer. While LLMs have thrived on abundant textual data, no comparable corpus exists for CUA trajectories. To address these gaps, we introduce FaraGen, a novel synthetic data generation system for multi-step web tasks. FaraGen can propose diverse tasks from frequently used websites, generate multiple solution attempts, and filter successful trajectories using multiple verifiers. It achieves high throughput, yield, and diversity for multi-step web tasks, producing verified trajectories at approximately $1 each. We use this data to train Fara-7B, a native CUA model that perceives the computer using only screenshots, executes actions via predicted coordinates, and is small enough to run on-device. We find that Fara-7B outperforms other CUA models of comparable size on benchmarks like WebVoyager, Online-Mind2Web, and WebTailBench -- our novel benchmark that better captures under-represented web tasks in pre-existing benchmarks. Furthermore, Fara-7B is competitive with much larger frontier models, illustrating key benefits of scalable data generation systems in advancing small efficient agentic models. We are making Fara-7B open-weight on Microsoft Foundry and HuggingFace, and we are releasing WebTailBench.

keyInsights: High-quality synthetic data, generated economically at approximately $1 per task by FaraGen, can effectively overcome the data bottleneck for CUAs and enable efficient small language models to achieve strong agentic capabilities.

Training a CUA with a "pixel-in, action-out" approach on diverse synthetic data leads to greater robustness and generalization across varied, dynamic real-world web interfaces compared to methods relying on structured accessibility trees.

Integrating explicit safety mechanisms during synthetic data generation (e.g., critical point identification) and model training (e.g., refusal data) is crucial for developing responsible and reliable AI agents capable of handling real-world consequences.
originalProblem: A severe data bottleneck exists for training robust Computer Use Agents (CUAs), as high-quality human-computer interaction data is scarce and expensive to collect.

Existing CUA models often rely on brittle accessibility trees, limiting generalization across dynamic real-world Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs), or are too large for efficient, on-device deployment.
Current CUA benchmarks lack comprehensive coverage of multi-step, complex, and under-represented real-world web tasks, making it difficult to accurately assess agent generalization.
results: FaraGen generated 145,603 verified trajectories (over 1 million steps across 70,117 unique domains) at an average cost of ~$1 per task, demonstrating economic feasibility for large-scale CUA data generation.

Fara-7B achieved a 73.5% success rate on WebVoyager and 38.4% on WebTailBench, outperforming other 7B-scale models and often surpassing larger frontier models (e.g., GPT-4o SoM) while reducing operational costs to ~$0.025 per task.

The model exhibited strong safety capabilities, with 94.2% refusal rates for harmful tasks on AgentHarm-Chat and 81.9% on WebTailBench-Refusals, and reliably stopped before critical actions in 19 out of 23 tasks on the REAL benchmark.
solution: Developed FaraGen, a novel synthetic data generation engine that autonomously proposes tasks, solves them via a multi-agent system, and verifies high-quality human-computer interaction trajectories.

Trained Fara-7B, a compact 7B-parameter native CUA model using a "pixel-in, action-out" paradigm, which perceives raw screenshots and browser metadata to predict low-level atomic actions, eliminating the need for accessibility trees at inference time.

Introduced WebTailBench, a new evaluation benchmark featuring 609 hand-verified tasks across 11 categories on live websites, specifically designed to cover complex, multi-step, and under-represented real-world scenarios, including safety-critical refusal tasks.
paper_summary.summary: Microsoft researchers introduce Fara-7B, a 7B-parameter Computer Use Agent (CUA), which was trained using FaraGen, a synthetic data generation engine producing over 145,000 interaction trajectories. This model achieves performance competitive with larger frontier models on benchmarks like WebVoyager (73.5% success rate) and WebTailBench (38.4% success rate) while operating at significantly lower costs of approximately $0.025 per task.

title: Fara-7B: An Efficient Agentic Model for Computer Use

abstract: Computer-Use Agents (CUA) are becoming increasingly capable of autonomously operating digital environments through Graphical User Interfaces (GUI). Yet, most GUI remain designed primarily for humans--prioritizing aesthetics and usability--forcing agents to adopt human-oriented behaviors that are unnecessary for efficient task execution. At the same time, rapid advances in coding-oriented language models (Coder) have transformed automatic GUI design. This raises a fundamental question: Can CUA as judges to assist Coder for automatic GUI design? To investigate, we introduce AUI-Gym, a benchmark for Automatic GUI development spanning 52 applications across diverse domains. Using language models, we synthesize 1560 tasks that simulate real-world scenarios. To ensure task reliability, we further develop a verifier that programmatically checks whether each task is executable within its environment. Building on this, we propose a Coder-CUA in Collaboration framework: the Coder acts as Designer, generating and revising websites, while the CUA serves as Judge, evaluating functionality and refining designs. Success is measured not by visual appearance, but by task solvability and CUA navigation success rate. To turn CUA feedback into usable guidance, we design a CUA Dashboard that compresses multi-step navigation histories into concise visual summaries, offering interpretable guidance for iterative redesign. By positioning agents as both designers and judges, our framework shifts interface design toward agent-native efficiency and reliability. Our work takes a step toward shifting agents from passive use toward active participation in digital environments. Our code and dataset are available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Show Lab, National University of Singapore

keyInsights: UI functional completeness is foundational for agents, and task solvability feedback significantly boosts it, leading to substantial gains (e.g., up to 31.4% in specific domains like 'app').
CUA navigation complexity within human-centric UIs is a primary bottleneck, and agent-centric iterative feedback effectively improves CUA Success Rates, particularly for less capable coders.
The CUA Dashboard provides superior, more efficient feedback compared to text-only or full screenshot methods, reducing visual token usage by an average of 70.4% while enhancing revision quality.
originalProblem: Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs) are traditionally designed for human users, leading to inefficiencies and complex navigation challenges for autonomous Computer-Use Agents (CUAs).
Coding-oriented language models, while capable of generating UIs, primarily produce designs for human consumption, lacking inherent optimization for agent reliability and task execution efficiency.
A systematic framework and scalable benchmark were needed to allow agents to actively participate in UI design and evaluation, moving beyond merely adapting to human-oriented environments.
results: Integrated revision, combining task solvability and CUA navigation feedback, consistently improved CUA Success Rate, with an average overall improvement of 6.8% and up to 11.7% for weaker coders.
Qualitative analysis revealed that agent-preferred UI designs prioritize de-stylization, increased contrast, simplified layouts, and clearer interactive elements over traditional human aesthetics.
The AUI-Gym's programmatic verifiers proved highly reliable for task evaluation, outperforming VLM-as-Judge approaches which showed low accuracy and F1-scores for classifying CUA outcomes.
solution: Introduced AUI-Gym, a novel and scalable benchmark comprising 52 web applications and 1,560 GPT-5-generated tasks, with programmatic verifiers to evaluate functional completeness and CUA solvability.

Proposed a Coder–CUA in Collaboration framework, establishing an iterative loop where Coders act as designers generating/revising UIs and CUAs act as judges providing task solvability and navigation feedback.

Developed the CUA Dashboard, an innovative mechanism that compresses complex CUA interaction trajectories into concise, visually-rich, and language-summarized feedback for effective UI redesign by Coders.
paper_summary.summary: This research establishes a framework for Computer-Use Agents (CUAs) to evaluate and iteratively refine user interface designs generated by coding-oriented language models. The approach, leveraging a new benchmark called AUI-Gym and a CUA Dashboard for feedback, leads to interfaces optimized for agent efficiency, improving CUA task success rates by an average of 6.8%.

title: Computer-Use Agents as Judges for Generative User Interface

abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have enabled computer use agents (CUAs) that operate GUIs autonomously, showing great potential, yet progress is limited by the lack of large-scale, open-source computer use data and foundation models. In this work, we introduce ScaleCUA, a step toward scaling open-source CUAs. It offers a large-scale dataset spanning 6 operating systems and 3 task domains, built via a closed-loop pipeline uniting automated agents with human experts. Trained on this scaled-up data, ScaleCUA can operate seamlessly across platforms. Specifically, it delivers strong gains over baselines (+26.6 on WebArena-Lite-v2, +10.7 on ScreenSpot-Pro) and sets new state-of-the-art results (94.4% on MMBench-GUI L1-Hard, 60.6% on OSWorld-G, 47.4% on WebArena-Lite-v2). These findings underscore the power of data-driven scaling for general-purpose computer use agents. We will release data, models, and code to advance future research: this https URL.

keyInsights: A large-scale, open-source, cross-platform, and multi-task GUI-centric dataset is crucial for training robust CUAs, enabling superior performance over proprietary and general VLMs in GUI-specific tasks.

The flexibility of three inference modes (Grounding, Direct Action, Reasoned Action) allows for versatile application, with the Reasoned Action mode consistently yielding higher success rates for complex, multi-step tasks at the cost of higher latency.

Ablation studies reveal that data augmentation, weak semantic trajectories, and raw coordinate representation are effective, while a delicate balance is needed when mixing general multimodal data with GUI-specific knowledge.
originalProblem: High-quality, in-domain computer-use data, particularly fine-grained action trajectories across diverse operating systems and applications, is exceptionally rare, costly to collect, and expensive to annotate.
Existing CUA datasets and models often have limited transferability across rapidly evolving software interfaces and operating systems, leading to quick obsolescence.
High-performing proprietary agents (e.g., those built on GPT-4o) remain closed-source, hindering broader academic research and development in the open-source community.
Current CUA architectures face trade-offs, with modular 'planner-grounder' paradigms suffering from high latency and 'native' agents constrained by data availability.
results: ScaleCUA-32B achieved a new state-of-the-art of 94.4% on the Hard Level of MMBench-GUI L1 for GUI understanding, outperforming all proprietary and open-source baselines.
For GUI grounding, ScaleCUA-32B attained an average score of 94.7% on ScreenSpot-v2 and 59.2% on ScreenSpot-Pro, consistently achieving state-of-the-art performance for open VLMs.

In end-to-end task completion, ScaleCUA-32B achieved the strongest Web performance on WebArena-Lite-v2, reaching 47.4% (50 steps) and outperforming the best native baseline by +26.0 points, also demonstrating strong performance in agentic workflows.
solution: Constructed a Cross-Platform Interactive Data Pipeline to efficiently collect high-quality GUI trajectories across six major platforms (Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS, Web) using both automated rule-driven agents and human experts.

Curated a large-scale, open-source dataset (ScaleCUA-Data) encompassing GUI understanding (471K examples), GUI grounding (17.1M samples), and task completion (19K trajectories) to overcome data scarcity.

Developed a family of scalable, versatile foundation models (ScaleCUA-3B, -7B, -32B) based on Qwen2.5-VL, supporting three inference paradigms: Grounding Mode, Direct Action Mode, and Reasoned Action Mode.
paper_summary.summary: The ScaleCUA project provides a large-scale, open-source, cross-platform dataset for training Computer Use Agents (CUAs) and introduces a family of versatile foundation models based on Qwen2.5-VL. ScaleCUA-32B achieved 94.4% on MMBench-GUI L1 (Hard Level) and 94.7% on ScreenSpot-v2, setting new state-of-the-art for open-source models in GUI understanding and grounding, and showing strong performance in end-to-end task completion.

title: ScaleCUA: Scaling Open-Source Computer Use Agents with Cross-Platform Data

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) based autonomous agents demonstrate multifaceted capabilities to contribute substantially to economic production. However, existing benchmarks remain focused on single agentic capability, failing to capture long-horizon real-world scenarios. Moreover, the reliance on human-in-the-loop feedback for realistic tasks creates a scalability bottleneck, hindering automated rollout collection and evaluation. To bridge this gap, we introduce AgencyBench, a comprehensive benchmark derived from daily AI usage, evaluating 6 core agentic capabilities across 32 real-world scenarios, comprising 138 tasks with specific queries, deliverables, and rubrics. These scenarios require an average of 90 tool calls, 1 million tokens, and hours of execution time to resolve. To enable automated evaluation, we employ a user simulation agent to provide iterative feedback, and a Docker sandbox to conduct visual and functional rubric-based assessment. Experiments reveal that closed-source models significantly outperform open-source models (48.4% vs 32.1%). Further analysis reveals significant disparities across models in resource efficiency, feedback-driven self-correction, and specific tool-use preferences. Finally, we investigate the impact of agentic scaffolds, observing that proprietary models demonstrate superior performance within their native ecosystems (e.g., Claude-4.5-Opus via Claude-Agent-SDK), while open-source models exhibit distinct performance peaks, suggesting potential optimization for specific execution frameworks. AgencyBench serves as a critical testbed for next-generation agents, highlighting the necessity of co-optimizing model architecture with agentic frameworks. We believe this work sheds light on the future direction of autonomous agents, and we release the full benchmark and evaluation toolkit at this https URL.

keyInsights: Proprietary LLM agents significantly outperform open-source counterparts, achieving an average score of 48.4% compared to 32.1% from open-source models on complex real-world tasks.

Agentic performance is not solely an intrinsic model property but is strongly influenced by the coupling between the LLM and its agentic scaffold, as evidenced by 'ecosystem synergy' for proprietary models.

Models exhibit distinct resource consumption patterns and problem-solving strategies, with some prioritizing 'brute-force' reasoning with high token usage (e.g., GPT-5.2) while others optimize for frugality (e.g., Grok-4.1-Fast).
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for autonomous agents often feature short-horizon tasks with limited interaction depth and tool calls, failing to capture real-world complexity.
Current benchmarks tend to focus on single agentic capabilities, inadequately assessing the multifaceted nature of real-world problems that demand a combination of skills.
Many complex agent evaluations still rely on human-in-the-loop feedback, creating scalability bottlenecks and hindering automated, reproducible assessment.
results: GPT-5.2 achieved the highest average score (56.5%) among proprietary models, while GLM-4.6 led open-source models (38.6%), demonstrating varying strengths across capabilities like game development and research.
Top-tier proprietary models showed superior feedback-driven self-correction, with GPT-5.2 exhibiting an 88.9% relative increase in pass rate (Pass@2 vs. Pass@1) after receiving feedback.

AGENCYBENCH tasks proved genuinely long-horizon, requiring an average of 1 million tokens, hours of execution time, and 90 tool calls per scenario, highlighting the demanding nature of real-world agentic challenges.
solution: AGENCYBENCH introduces 138 authentic tasks across 32 diverse real-world scenarios, demanding an average of 1 million tokens and 90 tool calls for resolution, covering six core agentic capabilities.
A unified automated evaluation framework was developed, employing a user simulation agent for iterative feedback and a Docker-based remote sandbox for visual and functional rubric-based assessment.
The benchmark tasks were meticulously constructed by 20 human experts, with an additional panel of four experts conducting a comprehensive review to ensure high quality and accuracy.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers introduced AGENCYBENCH, a comprehensive benchmark for LLM-based autonomous agents designed for 1M-token real-world contexts, enabling the rigorous evaluation of long-horizon tasks. It revealed a significant performance gap between proprietary (48.4% average score) and open-source (32.1%) models and highlighted varied efficiencies and self-correction capabilities across agents.

title: AgencyBench: Benchmarking the Frontiers of Autonomous Agents in 1M-Token Real-World Contexts

abstract: Modern coding scaffolds turn LLMs into capable software agents, but their ability to follow scaffold-specified instructions remains under-examined, especially when constraints are heterogeneous and persist across interactions. To fill this gap, we introduce OctoBench, which benchmarks scaffold-aware instruction following in repository-grounded agentic coding. OctoBench includes 34 environments and 217 tasks instantiated under three scaffold types, and is paired with 7,098 objective checklist items. To disentangle solving the task from following the rules, we provide an automated observation-and-scoring toolkit that captures full trajectories and performs fine-grained checks. Experiments on eight representative models reveal a systematic gap between task-solving and scaffold-aware compliance, underscoring the need for training and evaluation that explicitly targets heterogeneous instruction following. We release the benchmark to support reproducible benchmarking and to accelerate the development of more scaffold-aware coding agents.

keyInsights: A significant "scissors gap" exists between individual instruction compliance (high CSR) and holistic task success (low ISR), indicating models struggle with perfect concurrent adherence to all heterogeneous instructions.
Instruction-following performance varies by category, with "Memory" constraints showing higher compliance than "Skill" constraints, and many models lacking cross-scaffold robustness.

Models show diverse conflict resolution biases, often prioritizing System Prompts and User Queries over Project Documentation, but with significant variations for User Query vs. System Prompt conflicts.
originalProblem: Existing code generation benchmarks are outcome-oriented, measuring only final code correctness and overlooking adherence to non-functional or process constraints.

Current instruction-following (IF) benchmarks are often domain-agnostic and fail to capture the complex, heterogeneous, persistent, and potentially conflicting constraints inherent in real-world software development workflows.

LLM agents, while capable of coding, lack a robust evaluation method for their ability to meticulously follow diverse, scaffold-specified instructions distributed across various sources with varying priorities.
results: Models achieved a Check item Success Rate (CSR) of 79.75% to 85.64% for individual instructions, but only a 9.66% to 28.11% Instance Success Rate (ISR) for holistic instruction satisfaction, highlighting a major challenge in multi-constraint adherence.
"Skill" constraints proved to be a persistent bottleneck, with Claude-Opus-4.5 achieving 58.45% ISR and MiniMax-M2.1 dropping to 12.33% for this category.

Providing structured error feedback resulted in absolute ISR gains (e.g., ChatGLM-4.6 showed a 16.79% gain) and CSR increases (e.g., MiniMax-M2.1 saw a 5.54% increase), demonstrating the teachability of models.
solution: The researchers developed OCTOBENCH, a benchmark with 34 environments and 217 tasks across three agentic scaffold types, coupled with 7,098 binary objective checklist items.
It employs a checklist-based, LLM-as-a-judge automatic evaluation system to assess process-level instruction adherence by logging and normalizing agent trajectories.
A dedicated "OCTOBENCH-CONFLICT" dataset was created to specifically analyze how models resolve conflicting instructions from different sources.
paper_summary.summary: A new benchmark, OCTOBENCH, quantifies large language model agents' ability to follow diverse, scaffold-aware instructions in repository-grounded coding environments, revealing a substantial gap between individual instruction compliance and holistic adherence.

title: OctoBench: Benchmarking Scaffold-Aware Instruction Following in Repository-Grounded Agentic Coding

abstract: Tool-augmented large language models (LLMs), hereafter LLM agents, leverage external tools to solve diverse tasks and interface with the real world. However, current training practices largely rely on supervised fine-tuning (SFT) over static trajectories or reinforcement learning (RL) on narrow tasks, which generalize poorly beyond development settings and lead to brittleness with new tools and unseen workflows. Because code execution reflects many structural patterns of real-world workflows, we use coding problems as a structured substrate to build tool-use agent training environments with diverse task configurations. To this end, we introduce CodeGym, a scalable framework that synthesizes diverse, verifiable, and controllable multi-turn tool-use environments for agent RL, enabling LLM agents to explore and master various workflows actively. CodeGym converts static coding problems into interactive environments by extracting atomic functions or logic into callable tools, yielding verifiable tasks that span various tool-execution workflows. Models of varying sizes and chain-of-thought configurations trained in CodeGym exhibit consistent out-of-distribution generalizability; for example, Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct achieves an absolute accuracy gain of 8.7 points on the OOD benchmark $\tau$-Bench. These results highlight CodeGym as a step toward scalable general-purpose RL environments for training tool-use behaviors that align with real-world agent workflows.
title: Generalizable End-to-End Tool-Use RL with Synthetic CodeGym

abstract:
Despite the success in specific scenarios, existing foundation agents still
struggle to generalize across various virtual scenarios, mainly due to the
dramatically different encapsulations of environments with manually designed
observation and action spaces. To handle this issue, we propose the General
Computer Control (GCC) setting to restrict foundation agents to interact with
software through the most unified and standardized interface, i.e., using
screenshots as input and keyboard and mouse actions as output. We introduce
Cradle, a modular and flexible LMM-powered framework, as a preliminary attempt
towards GCC. Enhanced by six key modules, Cradle can understand input
screenshots and output executable code for low-level keyboard and mouse control
after high-level planning, so that Cradle can interact with any software and
complete long-horizon complex tasks without relying on any built-in APIs.
Experimental results show that Cradle exhibits remarkable generalizability and
impressive performance across four previously unexplored commercial video
games, five software applications, and a comprehensive benchmark, OSWorld.
Cradle is the first to enable foundation agents to follow the main storyline
and complete 40-minute-long real missions in the complex AAA game Red Dead
Redemption 2 (RDR2). Cradle can also create a city of a thousand people in
Cities: Skylines, farm and harvest parsnips in Stardew Valley, and trade and
bargain with a maximal weekly total profit of 87% in Dealer's Life 2. Cradle
can not only operate daily software, like Chrome, Outlook, and Feishu, but also
edit images and videos using Meitu and CapCut. Cradle greatly extends the reach
of foundation agents by enabling the easy conversion of any software,
especially complex games, into benchmarks to evaluate agents' various abilities
and facilitate further data collection, thus paving the way for generalist
agents.

keyInsights: Interacting with diverse computer environments solely via screenshots and keyboard/mouse actions (General Computer Control) enables foundation agents to operate across a vast range of previously inaccessible closed-source software and commercial games.

A modular framework leveraging Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) like GPT-4o, augmented with external tools for spatial grounding (e.g., Grounding DINO & SAM) and robust memory mechanisms, is effective for multi-modal alignment, long-horizon reasoning, and precise low-level control.

While LMMs provide strong reasoning and visual understanding, they currently exhibit limitations in precise spatial perception, Out-of-Distribution (OOD) icon recognition, and robust UI understanding, especially in dynamic or pixel-art environments.
originalProblem: Existing foundation agents struggle to generalize across diverse virtual environments due to reliance on environment-specific APIs, DOM trees, or pre-defined semantic action spaces.

The dependency on internal information limits the applicability of AI agents to countless closed-source commercial software and games, hindering the creation of truly universal benchmarks and the exploration of rich digital training grounds.
Current methods often require extensive, costly human expert demonstration data for training, making generalization to new, complex environments inefficient.
results: CRADLE successfully completed core missions in complex AAA game Red Dead Redemption 2 (e.g., Chapters I and II missions, total 40 minutes) without relying on game-specific APIs, and cultivated crops over multiple in-game days in Stardew Valley.

The framework achieved high success rates in commercial tasks like 'Weekly Shop Management' in Dealer's Life 2 (93.6% transactions finalized, avg. +39.6% profit) and successfully built cities in Cities: Skylines, reaching an average maximal population of 450.
